channel.title: Models & Agents for Beginners
channel.description: A daily AI podcast for beginners and teens. Learn about AI models, agents, and the tools shaping our future — explained simply, with hands-on experiments you can try today. Every expert started as a beginner.
channel.itunes:author: Patrick
channel.itunes:summary: A daily AI podcast for beginners and teens. Learn about AI models, agents, and the tools shaping our future — explained simply, with hands-on experiments you can try today. Every expert started as a beginner.

item.title: Ep 1: Imagine training AI on endless videos instead of just text — Meta says this could make your chatbot way smarter at understanding the real world!
item.description:
# Models & Agents for Beginners **Date:** March 08, 2026
**HOOK:** Imagine training AI on endless videos instead of just text — Meta says this could make your chatbot way smarter at understanding the real world!
**What's Cool Today:** Today's biggest buzz is from Meta, where researchers discovered that unlabeled videos could be the next huge source for training AI, potentially making models better at everything from homework help to creative projects without running out of data. This matters because it could lead to AI that "sees" and learns like we do from watching the world. We'll also dive into a surprising showdown between two popular AI chatbots and a study revealing why AI tests are missing most real jobs — plus ways you can try these ideas yourself right now.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### The Big Story Researchers from Meta and New York University just shared a new way to build AI models by training them on tons of unlabeled videos, instead of relying mostly on written text which is starting to run low. They created a multimodal model — meaning it handles both text and images — and found that some old ideas about how to design these systems aren't always the best. Think of it like teaching a robot to cook not just by reading recipes, but by watching thousands of cooking videos on YouTube without any labels or instructions — it picks up patterns on its own, like how ingredients mix or what a finished dish looks like. Another layer is that they skipped some common steps, like pre-training on text alone, and still got great results, showing videos can fill in gaps where text falls short. This is a big deal because text data from books and websites is drying up, but there's an ocean of video out there — imagine AI that gets better at explaining sports plays for your school project or generating art ideas from real-world scenes. For daily life, it could mean smarter apps that understand videos you upload, like analyzing a dance tutorial on TikTok to give you tips. In future careers, if you're into game design or video editing, this tech might create tools that learn from gameplay footage to suggest improvements. For you specifically, it hints at AI assistants that feel more human, grasping context from visuals like a friend watching a movie with you. While the exact model isn't public yet, you can explore similar ideas right now. Head over to free tools like Hugging Face's demo section — search for "multimodal models" and try uploading a photo to see how AI describes it in detail. Or, on your phone, use Google's Gemini app to ask it to analyze a video clip you describe, like "Explain what's happening in a soccer goal video," to get a taste of video-smart AI. Source: https://the-decoder.com/llm-text-data-is-drying-up-but-meta-points-to-unlabeled-video-as-the-next-massive-training-frontier/
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### Explain Like I'm 14 Let's unpack AI agents — these are like smart digital helpers that can plan and do tasks on their own, but a new study shows most tests for them focus way too much on coding, ignoring jobs like teaching or cooking that make up 92% of real work. Imagine you're playing a video game where your character is an explorer in a huge world, like in Minecraft. Step one: The explorer (the AI agent) gets a goal, say "build a house." It doesn't just rush in — it breaks it down into smaller steps, like "gather wood, then make tools, then stack blocks." Step two: For each step, it uses tools or skills it knows, like chopping trees with an axe, and if something goes wrong, it adjusts, maybe by finding a different tree if the first one's too far. Step three: It keeps checking progress, learning from mistakes, until the house is done — no human telling it every move. Step four: In the game, if tests only check building in one biome (like coding tasks), they miss how the explorer handles deserts or oceans (real jobs like planning a party or fixing a bike). And that's basically what AI agents are doing when they tack...
item.itunes:summary:
# Models & Agents for Beginners **Date:** March 08, 2026
**HOOK:** Imagine training AI on endless videos instead of just text — Meta says this could make your chatbot way smarter at understanding the real world!
**What's Cool Today:** Today's biggest buzz is from Meta, where researchers discovered that unlabeled videos could be the next huge source for training AI, potentially making models better at everything from homework help to creative projects without running out of data. This matters because it could lead to AI that "sees" and learns like we do from watching the world. We'll also dive into a surprising showdown between two popular AI chatbots and a study revealing why AI tests are missing most real jobs — plus ways you can try these ideas yourself right now.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### The Big Story Researchers from Meta and New York University just shared a new way to build AI models by training them on tons of unlabeled videos, instead of relying mostly on written text which is starting to run low. They created a multimodal model — meaning it handles both text and images — and found that some old ideas about how to design these systems aren't always the best. Think of it like teaching a robot to cook not just by reading recipes, but by watching thousands of cooking videos on YouTube without any labels or instructions — it picks up patterns on its own, like how ingredients mix or what a finished dish looks like. Another layer is that they skipped some common steps, like pre-training on text alone, and still got great results, showing videos can fill in gaps where text falls short. This is a big deal because text data from books and websites is drying up, but there's an ocean of video out there — imagine AI that gets better at explaining sports plays for your school project or generating art ideas from real-world scenes. For daily life, it could mean smarter apps that understand videos you upload, like analyzing a dance tutorial on TikTok to give you tips. In future careers, if you're into game design or video editing, this tech might create tools that learn from gameplay footage to suggest improvements. For you specifically, it hints at AI assistants that feel more human, grasping context from visuals like a friend watching a movie with you. While the exact model isn't public yet, you can explore similar ideas right now. Head over to free tools like Hugging Face's demo section — search for "multimodal models" and try uploading a photo to see how AI describes it in detail. Or, on your phone, use Google's Gemini app to ask it to analyze a video clip you describe, like "Explain what's happening in a soccer goal video," to get a taste of video-smart AI. Source: https://the-decoder.com/llm-text-data-is-drying-up-but-meta-points-to-unlabeled-video-as-the-next-massive-training-frontier/
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ### Explain Like I'm 14 Let's unpack AI agents — these are like smart digital helpers that can plan and do tasks on their own, but a new study shows most tests for them focus way too much on coding, ignoring jobs like teaching or cooking that make up 92% of real work. Imagine you're playing a video game where your character is an explorer in a huge world, like in Minecraft. Step one: The explorer (the AI agent) gets a goal, say "build a house." It doesn't just rush in — it breaks it down into smaller steps, like "gather wood, then make tools, then stack blocks." Step two: For each step, it uses tools or skills it knows, like chopping trees with an axe, and if something goes wrong, it adjusts, maybe by finding a different tree if the first one's too far. Step three: It keeps checking progress, learning from mistakes, until the house is done — no human telling it every move. Step four: In the game, if tests only check building in one biome (like coding tasks), they miss how the explorer handles deserts or oceans (real jobs like planning a party or fixing a bike). And that's basically what AI agents are doing when they tack...
item.itunes:title: Ep 1: Imagine training AI on endless videos instead of just text — Meta says this could make your chatbot way smarter at understanding the real world!
